name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
كُتِبَ عَلَيْكُمْ إِذَا حَضَرَ أَحَدَكُمُ ٱلْمَوْتُ إِن تَرَكَ خَيْرًا ٱلْوَصِيَّةُ لِلْوَٰلِدَيْنِ وَٱلْأَقْرَبِينَ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ ۖ حَقًّا عَلَى ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ (١٨٠)
```

```english
Prescribed for you, when death approaches any of you and he leaves behind any property, is that he make a bequest for his parents and relatives, in an honourable manner—an obligation on the Godwary. (180)
```

```arabic
فَمَنۢ بَدَّلَهُۥ بَعْدَ مَا سَمِعَهُۥ فَإِنَّمَآ إِثْمُهُۥ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ يُبَدِّلُونَهُۥٓ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ (١٨١)
```

```english
And should anyone alter it after hearing it, its sin shall indeed lie on those who alter it. Indeed Allah is all-hearing, all-knowing. (181)
```

```arabic
فَمَنْ خَافَ مِن مُّوصٍ جَنَفًا أَوْ إِثْمًا فَأَصْلَحَ بَيْنَهُمْ فَلَآ إِثْمَ عَلَيْهِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (١٨٢)
```

```english
But should someone, fearing deviance or sin on the testator’s behalf, set things right between them, there is no sin upon him. Indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (182)
```

## (Commentary)

**His saying, the Exalted:** **كُتِبَ عَلَيْكُمْ إِذَا حَضَرَ أَحَدَكُمُ الْمَوْتُ إِن تَرَكَ خَيْرًا الْوَصِيَّةُ** "Prescribed for you, when death approaches any of you and he leaves behind any property, is the making of a bequest" (2:180). The phrasing of the verse is that of obligation (الوجوب), for the term "prescribing" (الكتابة) is used in the Quran in contexts of decisiveness and necessity (اللزوم). This is further supported by His saying at the end of the verse, "as a duty" (حقاً), for a "duty" (الحق), like "prescribing," implies the meaning of necessity. However, the qualification of this duty with His saying, "upon the God-conscious" (على المتقين), weakens the indication of a strict obligation or a firm decree (العزيمة); it would have been more appropriate for an obligation to say, "a duty upon the believers." In any case, it has been said that the verse is abrogated (منسوخة) by the verse of inheritance. Even if that were so, what is abrogated is the mandatory nature (الفرض), not the recommendation (الندب) or the inherent desirability of the act. Perhaps the qualification of the duty to the "God-conscious" (المتقين) — *those who possess godwariness (التقوى), the inner shield of consciousness that protects one from Divine displeasure* — in the verse is intended to convey this purpose.

What is meant by "property" (الخير) is wealth (المال), and it appears to refer to substantial wealth that is of significant value, rather than a small amount that is considered negligible. What is meant by "honorable manner" (المعروف) is the recognized and customary practice of kindness and benevolence.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَمَن بَدَّلَهُ بَعْدَ مَا سَمِعَهُ فَإِنَّمَا إِثْمُهُ عَلَى الَّذِينَ يُبَدِّلُونَهُ** "Then whoever alters it after hearing it, its sin shall rest only on those who alter it" (2:181). The pronoun in "its sin" (إثمه) refers back to the act of "altering" (التبديل), while the remaining pronouns refer to the "bequest in an honorable manner" (الوصية بالمعروف). The latter is a verbal noun (مصدر) for which both grammatical possibilities are permissible. He said, "on those who alter it" (على الذين يبدلونه), rather than simply "on them," to indicate the cause of the sin, which is the alteration of the honorable bequest, and to allow the subsequent verse to follow logically from it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَمَنْ خَافَ مِن مُّوصٍ جَنَفًا أَوْ إِثْمًا فَأَصْلَحَ بَيْنَهُمْ فَلَا إِثْمَ عَلَيْهِ** "But should someone fear partiality or sin on the part of a testator, and settle the matter between them, there is no sin upon him" (2:182). "Partiality" (الجنف) is inclination and deviation. It is said that it refers to the outward turning of the feet, just as *al-hanaf* (الحنف) with the letter *ḥā’* refers to their inward turning. In any case, what is meant here is an inclination toward sin, as evidenced by the context of "sin" (الإثم). The verse is a derivation from the preceding one. The meaning—and Allah knows best—is that the sin of alteration falls only upon those who alter an honorable bequest. From this it follows that if someone fears that the testator's bequest involves sin or an inclination toward it, and he rectifies the matter between the parties by returning it to that which contains no sin, then there is no sin upon him. This is because he has not altered an honorable bequest; rather, he has only altered that which contained sin or partiality.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Kafi*, *al-Tahdhib*, and the *Tafsir* of al-Ayyashi—the wording being from the latter—it is narrated from Muhammad ibn Muslim, from Imam al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام: "I asked him whether a bequest is permissible for an heir. He replied: 'Yes,' then he recited this verse: **إن ترك خيراً الوصية للوالدين والأقربين** ('If he leaves behind property, the bequest is for parents and relatives')."

In the *Tafsir* of al-Ayyashi, from Imam al-Sadiq, from his father, from Imam Ali عليه‌السلام, who said: "Whoever does not make a bequest at the time of his death for those of his relatives who do not inherit has sealed his deeds with a sin."

Also in the *Tafsir* of al-Ayyashi, from Imam al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام regarding the verse, he said: "It is a right (حق) that Allah has placed in the wealth of the people for the Master of this Affair (صاحب هذا الأمر)." I said: "Is there a defined limit for that?" He said: "Yes." I asked: "How much?" He replied: "The minimum is a sixth and the maximum is a third."

**I say:** Al-Saduq also narrated this meaning in *al-Faqih* from him عليه‌السلام. This is a subtle derivation from the verse when joined with the saying of the Exalted: **النَّبِيُّ أَوْلَىٰ بِالْمُؤْمِنِينَ مِنْ أَنفُسِهِمْ وَأَزْوَاجُهُ أُمَّهَاتُهُمْ وَأُولُو الْأَرْحَامِ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلَىٰ بِبَعْضٍ فِي كِتَابِ اللَّهِ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَالْمُهَاجِرِينَ إِلَّا أَن تَفْعَلُوا إِلَىٰ أَوْلِيَائِكُم مَّعْرُوفًا كَانَ ذَٰلِكَ فِي الْكِتَابِ مَسْطُورًا** "The Prophet is closer to the faithful than their own souls, and his wives are their mothers. The blood relatives are closer to each other in the Book of Allah than the [other] faithful and the Emigrants, unless you should do some kindness to your friends. That is written in the Book" (33:6). This verse is the one that abrogated the ruling of inheritance through brotherhood that existed at the beginning of Islam; it negated inheritance through brotherhood and established it for kinship. Then it excepted the "kindness" (معروف) done for the "guardians" (الأولياء). The Prophet has been counted as a guardian (ولي), and the pure ones from his progeny are guardians for them. This excepted "kindness" is the subject of His saying, the Exalted: "If he leaves behind property, the bequest..."—and they are the near of kin—so understand this.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Ayyashi, from one of the two Imams (al-Baqir or al-Sadiq) عليهما السلام regarding the saying of the Exalted, "Prescribed for you, when death approaches..." the verse. He عليه‌السلام said: "It is abrogated; it was abrogated by the verse of fixed shares (آية الفرائض), which are the inheritances."

**I say:** The way to reconcile the previous narrations with this one is that what is abrogated from the verse is only the obligation (الوجوب), while the recommendation (الاستحباب) remains as it is.

In *al-Majma'*, from Imam al-Baqir عليه‌السلام regarding His saying, "But should someone fear partiality or sin on the part of a testator..." the verse. He said: "Partiality (الجنف) is when it is done by way of error, where he does not know what is permissible."

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, Imam al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام said: "When a man makes his bequest, it is not permissible for the executor (الوصي) to change any bequest he makes; rather, he must carry it out as he bequeathed, unless he bequeaths something other than what Allah commanded, thereby sinning in the bequest and acting unjustly. In that case, it is permissible for the executor to return it to the truth. For example, a man who has heirs might leave all the wealth to some of his heirs and deprive others; the executor is permitted to return it to the truth, and that is His saying: 'partiality or sin' (جنفاً أو إثماً). Partiality (الجنف) is the inclination toward some heirs over others, and sin (الإثم) is to command the building of fire-temples or the production of intoxicants; it is permissible for the executor not to act upon any of that."

**I say:** From the meaning of "partiality" (الجنف) mentioned in the narration, the meaning of His saying, "and settle the matter between them" (فأصلح بينهم), becomes clear. What is meant is settling the matter between the heirs due to the occurrence of a dispute among them arising from the partiality of the testator.

In *al-Kafi*, from Muhammad ibn Suqah, who said: "I asked Imam al-Baqir عليه‌السلام about the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic: 'Then whoever alters it after hearing it, its sin shall rest only on those who alter it.' He said: 'It was abrogated by the one after it, His saying: "But should someone fear partiality or sin on the part of a testator, and settle the matter between them, there is no sin upon him."' He said: 'It means the executor (الموصى إليه); if he fears partiality from the testator regarding his children in what he bequeathed to him—in that which does not please Allah and is contrary to the truth—then there is no sin upon him, meaning upon the executor, to alter it toward the truth and toward that which pleases Allah from the path of truth.'"

**I say:** This is an instance of explaining the verse by the verse (تفسير الآية بالآية). Applying the term "abrogation" (النسخ) to it is not according to the technical definition (الاصطلاح). It has been mentioned previously that in their speech, "abrogation" is sometimes applied to meanings other than those defined by the scholars of the principles of jurisprudence (الأصوليون).
